text: And we are back for another episode of This is My Architecture here on the showroom floor at AWS Summit in Sydney.
And today we have a very special guest, someone who I've always loved to have the same title of.
We've got Dr. Daniel Bassett from TASOLS who's the Chief Data Officer.
Yep, yep.
Welcome to This is My Architecture.
Thanks.
So Daniel, tell us a little bit about what TASOLS does.
Okay, so TASOLS started 17 years ago and they were dealing with budgeting and forecasting in the finance sector.
And then they started to move more naturally into analytics and business intelligence.
And more recently we've done the more advanced analytics and data science.
And so that's the team that I lead.
So I'm guessing you guys know a fair bit about data.
Yeah.
Tell us a little bit about analytics and business intelligence.
It's a pretty traditional domain, but we're starting to hear a lot more customers wanting to understand machine learning.
And I understand that's where you guys are really doing a lot of work.
Yeah, so with business intelligence they'll tend to show KPIs and dashboards of things that they already know.
What we tend to do is we go into a business and we try to answer their questions that they don't know using machine learning and statistics.
And then once we've discovered that, that's when you bring in the BI and the business intelligence to display that.
So it's really all about getting that insight.
Yeah.
Daniel, you've got a number of AWS services.
I can't wait.
Let's dig into it.
Yeah.
Okay.
So what we wanted to do originally is that we work with multiple businesses and businesses always have their own requirements in terms of their data.
So we wanted to give them an option of the different data sources here.
So we can use CSVs, traditional databases, big data.
And then when we transform the data, that's when we get it ready for the machine learning models and we bring it into the S3.
And that's when we kind of kick off the main part of the framework.
So you're saying customers can go and drop their flat files in places like S3 or EFS.
But if they've got structured, you know, column databases that can do be in general straight into Redshift.
Fantastic.
So then you're using Glue to do some ETL, right?
Yeah, that's right.
Yeah.
And tell us about what's going on here, because this is really the heart of the solution.
Okay, so what we wanted to do here is we wanted to create like an object-oriented kit set type deal where we can, because one of the powers of AWS is chaining those machine learning models together.
And so what we did is we created, basically, this would be one chain, and then we can bring in multiple chains, which we'll probably talk about soon.
But so you would bring the data into the S3, and then that would create a data load event.
And then what that day load event would then trigger this Lambda function.
And then the Lambda function will fire off SageMaker, and then SageMaker will run the model and then insert the data back into the S3.
So, Daniel, I don't see any EC2 instances.
I don't see containers.
This is a complete serverless solution.
Exactly.
So this is really, like really a full-blown event-driven architecture for machine learning.
Exactly.
We did actually originally do it in the EC2, but we found that it was just much better in terms of just automation and productionizing to use the serverless architecture.
Okay.
I really want to dig into how you're using SageMaker because I love the fact that you're actually layering lots of different machine learning models together.
So tell us a little bit about which models and algorithms you're using in each of these different phases.
Okay.
So we mainly do two models.
So we'll run this for the first run-through.
We'll do the k-means clustering models.
And so that will cluster customers into the different groups.
And then the second run-through, once they've been clustered into the different groups, will be the more prediction models.
That's when we'll be using random models.
Why do you cluster all of that data into groups?
Okay.
Because if you just ran one model across the whole data, you might find a feature is really, really important.
But if you actually cluster those into different groups, that feature might be only important for groups two and three, but not one.
So it gives you a lot more power and a lot more.
You're able to really hone in on what's important to each group.
Got you.
Got you.
What about predict?
Okay.

So for the prediction, that's when we would be running the random forest models, sometimes linear regression on those customer groups to find out what features were important in determining the next best offer.
Tell me about how you train the data.
So we tended to use the random forest models to train the data because mostly what we've found with business data is it's very non-parametric.
So you'll get a lot of errors.
It's not kind of this linear data.
So the random forest seems to be the best model for that.
Fantastic.
But the training was really, really quick and easy to implement.
And this is really what the insight that you come out with your customers is that next best offer, right?
Yeah, correct.

What we would do with that is when we've run those prediction models, they will produce feature weights, which will basically show you from top to bottom what are the most important things in driving that next best offer.
And then you can use them as the next best offer essentially.
Fantastic.
So how do your customers actually consume all of this?
Okay.
So there's basically two ways that we've found.
And again, this is the same at this end as this end.
We wanted to give them what they wanted.
So the two ways would be through a BI dashboard and then another one would be through an API.
So the API would be someone calls into a call center.
They would have that customer information.
They would then call to the machine learning model, which would then say, this is safe.
You're looking at churn.
This customer is likely to churn and then it would offer you the next best offer.
So that means that your customers could actually embed this as an API call for their websites or their real-time applications.
But you could also give business users a full-blown analytics historical view of all this.
And they're two different things because management would probably be really interested in the business intelligence, whereas the API is more for actually actioning something.
Daniel, this is an amazing architecture for how you've just coupled so many different elements.
I love how you're layering all of the different uses of SageMaker together to actually really refine the output for your customers.
What's next?
What's next is that we want to really bring in more of those software development tools that's just lacking in the machine learning space.
So we want to use CodePipeline to get that continuous integration, versioning control,
so that we've already automated it, but it's just fine-tuning it that little bit further.
Fantastic.
You know, an event-driven machine learning architecture like this,
I can really see how a lot of customers can really apply it into lots of different domains.
Daniel, thank you so much for coming and sharing your architecture with us.
And thank you for joining us on another episode of This Is My Architecture Live.
